title: Wetland Loss Detection And Migratory Bird Population Impact
summary: Build a verified workflow for linking satellite-detected wetland loss at specific stopover sites along major migratory flyways to waterbird population trends, with explicit wetland-bird linkage methodology, flyway-scale data-coverage assessment, and multi-threat attribution documentation.
why_it_matters: An estimated 35 percent of global wetlands have been lost since 1970, with the fastest losses in Asia and Africa. Shorebird populations on the East Asian-Australasian Flyway have declined by over 50 percent since the 1990s, primarily due to intertidal wetland loss in the Yellow Sea. Yet the analytical chain from satellite-detected wetland change at specific stopover sites to flyway-scale population impact has not been systematically built. Without verified wetland-bird linkage analysis, conservation prioritization relies on expert judgment that may miss critical unprotected stopover sites.

success_metrics.target: Every wetland-loss claim links to satellite product, resolution, temporal coverage, and wetland-type classification methodology.
success_metrics.validation_method: Schema validation and reviewer spot-checks against satellite wetland-change documentation.

success_metrics.target: Every bird-population trend claim documents International Waterbird Census and eBird data coverage for the analyzed flyway, including site-monitoring gaps and temporal coverage.
success_metrics.validation_method: Reviewer confirms data-coverage assessment for each flyway analyzed.

success_metrics.target: No wetland-loss-to-bird-decline attribution is merged without documenting other threats across the migratory range (hunting, pollution, climate change, disturbance).
success_metrics.validation_method: Red-team reviewer confirms every attribution claim has explicit multi-threat caveats.

review_policy.merge_gate: Schema validation, domain review, multi-threat attribution safety review, and replication for quantitative outputs.
